text:
Time is the invisible thread that connects all of our moments together. We experience it as a constant flow, always moving forward, never pausing, never reversing. Yet our relationship with time is deeply paradoxical. When we're engaged in something we love, time seems to accelerate and compress—hours vanish like minutes. When we're bored or suffering, time stretches unbearably, each second becoming an eternity.

We spend much of our lives managing time, dividing it into schedules and deadlines, as though we could master it through organization. We fear wasting it, hoarding it, trying to optimize every moment. But time resists this impulse. No amount of productivity systems can actually slow it down or make it expand. The harder we grasp at it, the faster it seems to slip away.

Perhaps the deepest truth about time is that it teaches us about impermanence. Everything we build, everything we achieve, everything we cherish will eventually fade. This could be depressing, but many find it liberating instead. If time is finite and unstoppable, then what matters most is not how much we have but what we do with it. It pushes us toward authenticity, toward spending our days in ways that genuinely matter to us rather than in ways we think we should spend them.

Time also connects us to others. Shared time—a conversation with a friend, time spent with family—becomes the substance of relationship. Memory preserves moments from the past and makes them present again in our minds. Looking forward, anticipation fills the future with meaning. In this way, time is not just something that happens to us; it's the medium through which we live and love and build meaning.
